figures.caption: Table 2: Summary of experiments discussed in Section 3.

Figure 1: Linear operators mapping mean profiles to fluxes under (a–c) stable and (d–f) unstable conditions. Each matrix row encodes an algebraic stencil resembling the overall operations, such as the vertical derivative.

Figure 2: Momentum flux hodographs showing the relation between the turbulent flux components w′u′ and w′v′ and its variability with height for (a) stable and (b) unstable boundary layer conditions. Solid blue curves denote LES reference values, while dashed orange curves correspond to predictions from the learned operators. Black markers indicate surface values.

Figure 3: multivariate operators for the stable ABL and their reconstruction skill for the stable boundary layer experiment (∂θ/∂tz=0 = −1Kh−1). Using (θ, u, v) jointly to predict each flux markedly reduces errors relative to scalar-only operators and better captures nonlocal interactions.

Figure 4: Multivariate operators for the convective ABL and their reconstruction skill for the convective boundary layer experiment (w′θ′z=0 = 50W/m2). Using (θ, u, v) jointly to predict each flux reduces errors relative to scalar-only operators and better captures nonlocal interactions.

Figure 5: Time-height plots of (a) temperature, (b) u-velocity, and (c) v-velocity for the stable boundary layer case, comparing LES (left), a posteriori SCM using the data-driven parameterization (center), and their difference (right). The parameterization tracks the LES to within ±1 K for temperature and ∼ 10% of geostrophic wind for velocities, while reproducing canonical features such as Ekman turning. Results are shown for the multivariate parameterization of Equation 9.
Figure 6: Time-height plots of (a) temperature, (b) u-velocity, and (c) v-velocity for the stable boundary layer using the KPP scheme.

Figure 7: Time-height plots of (a) potential temperature, (b) u-velocity, and (c) v-velocity for the unstable boundary layer (w′θ′|z=0 = 50W m−2), comparing (left) LES, (middle) a posteriori SCM results through (right) their difference.

Figure 8: Instantaneous snapshots of temperature, u-velocity and v-velocity profiles at select times for (a) LES data, (b) learned operator, and (c) interpolated operator for the stable ABL with ∂θ/∂t|z=0 = −1.5K h−1.

Figure 9: Instantaneous snapshots of temperature, u-velocity and v-velocity profiles at select times for (a) LES data, (b) learned operator, and (c) interpolated operator for the unstable ABL with w′θ′|z=0 = 100W m−2.

Figure 10: Time-height plots of temperature, u-, and v-velocity for a posteriori tests initialized from neutral stability. Shown are the cases for (a) transition to a stable ABL with ∂θ/∂t = −1K h−1, and (b) transition to an unstable ABL with w′θ′ = 150W m−2.

Figure 11: Snapshots of potential temperature and mean u/v during abrupt flips of surface forcing. (a) Unstable→ stable: progressive surface cooling, growth of a capping inversion, and emergence of a low-level jet with inertial oscillations within ∼3 h. (b) Stable→unstable: rapid inversion breakup, formation and deepening of a convective mixed layer, and inertially modulated wind adjustment with a transient wind maximum near 0.2–0.3Lz. In both directions, the a posteriori SCM with learned operators remains numerically stable and reproduces the expected adjustment pathways.
